Die Erfahrungen von Siemens Healthineers zeigen, dass eine frühzeitige Einbindung, klare Strukturen und vielfältige Kommunikation entscheidend sind. 

Was ich wichtig finde ist auch folgendes: Change Management erweist sich auch bei primär technischen Projekten als wichtiger Erfolgsfaktor. Die Handlungsfelder sind vielleicht etwas anders. So gibt es beispielsweise wenig End-User Training. Aber Änderungen die Prozesse, Jobs und Aufgaben betreffen gibt es auch bei technischen Projekten. Und auch Fragen zum Mehrwert eines solchen technischen Projekts und die Besprechung und Kommunikation dieser Vorteile. Und hier ist eben Change Management sehr wichtig mit all seinen Facetten, von Stakeholder Analysen bis Kommunikation

⁉️ Habt ihr weitere Beispiele, was bei sehr technischen Projekten besonders ist rund um Change Management? Freue mich auf Kommentare hier auch. 

Shownotes

Buch: Die 5 Sprachen der Mitarbeitermotivation

Buch: Der Change Code von Dieter Lederer

Doku der Community-Session mit Anja zum SAP Change Starterkit

SAP News: Siemens Healthineers entscheidet sich für RISE with SAP um Leistung zu steigern

Aus-und Weiterbildung bei Siemens Healthineers

entry.title: Live Sessions im SAP Learning Hub: Interaktiv SAP lernen
entry.content:
Was sind Live Sessions im SAP Learning Hub?

Live Sessions sind Online-Veranstaltungen, die in der Regel ein bis zwei Stunden dauern und von SAP-Experten geleitet werden. Sie finden das ganze Jahr über statt und decken ein breites Spektrum an SAP-Themen ab. Im Jahr 2024 haben über 200.000 Lernende dieses Angebot genutzt. Um möglichst viele Teilnehmende weltweit zu erreichen, werden die Sessions in zwei Zeitzonen und in englischer Sprache angeboten.

Die Live Sessions haben verschiedene Formate. Sie sind zu einem Großteil

  • Expert Deep Dives zu bestimmten SAP Themen.

Darüber hinaus gibt es

  • What’s New Sessions,
  • Stay Certified Sessions und
  • Get Started Sessions.

Gerade die Deep Dives und die Get Started Sessions haben eine auf Interaktion und Kollaboration ausgerichtetes Set up, das eine gute Interaktion zwischen Lernenden und Experten ermöglicht.

Vorteile der Live-Sessions im SAP Learning Hub

Diese Live-Sessions bieten eine Vielzahl von Vorteilen:

  1. Expertenwissen: Aktuelles Expertenwissen: SAP-Fachleute präsentieren die neuesten Themen und Technologien. Dies gewährleistet, dass Teilnehmer stets über die aktuellsten Entwicklungen und Produkte informiert sind.
  2. Interaktivität: Teilnehmer können in Echtzeit Fragen stellen und Diskussionen führen. Dies fördert nicht nur das Verständnis in komplexe Sachverhalte, sondern ermöglicht auch den Austausch von Erfahrungen und Best Practices. Auch können sie von Fragen anderer lernen.
  3. Praxisorientierung: Die Sessions konzentrieren sich oft auf reale Anwendungsfälle. Dies hilft den Teilnehmenden, das Gelernte direkt in ihrem Arbeitsalltag umzusetzen.
  4. Networking: Teilnehmer haben die Möglichkeit, sich mit Gleichgesinnten aus der SAP-Community auszutauschen. Dies fördert den Wissensaustausch und kann zu wertvollen beruflichen Kontakten führen.
  5. Flexibilität: Die Sessions werden zu verschiedenen Zeiten angeboten, um unterschiedliche Zeitzonen und Arbeitszeiten zu berücksichtigen. Zudem sind viele Sessions auch als Aufzeichnung verfügbar.
  6. Kostengünstigkeit: Für Abonnenten des SAP Learning Hub sind die Live Sessions im Abonnement enthalten, was eine kosteneffiziente Möglichkeit zur kontinuierlichen Weiterbildung darstellt.
  7. Aktualität der Themen: Die Themen der Live Sessions spiegeln die aktuellen Trends und Entwicklungen in der SAP-Welt wider. In jüngster Zeit gab es beispielsweise Sessions zu: SAP S/4HANA Cloud - SAP Business Technology Platform - SAP Analytics Cloud - Integration verschiedener SAP-Lösungen und viele Sessions zu Artificial Intelligence.

Die Live Sessions im SAP Learning Hub ergänzen die on-demand Lerninhalte optimal und bieten SAP-Profis die Möglichkeit, ihr Wissen kontinuierlich zu erweitern und auf dem neuesten Stand zu bleiben. Sie sind ein wichtiger Baustein für die professionelle Entwicklung in der sich schnell wandelnden Welt der SAP-Technologien.

Für Interessierte empfiehlt es sich, regelmäßig das Angebot im SAP Learning Hub zu prüfen und relevante Live Sessions in die persönliche Weiterbildungsplanung einzubeziehen. Bei der Auswahl der relevanten Live Sessions helfen die Selektionsfilter wie Level (Vorkenntnisse) oder Rolle des Lernenden.

Die Kombination aus flexiblem Online-Lernen und interaktiven Live-Veranstaltungen macht den SAP Learning Hub zu einer umfassenden Ressource für alle, die in der SAP-Welt up-to-date bleiben möchten.

Das Live-Session-Angebot wird unter learning.sap.com veröffentlicht und kontinuierlich auf alle aktuellen Produktbereiche ausgeweitet. Hier finden Sie die die kommenden Live Sessions.

Für regelmäßige Updates zum Lernangebot der SAP empfehlen wir, den SAP Training Newsletter zu abonnieren.

Wir freuen uns über Fragen und Anmerkungen in den Kommentaren.

entry.title: Live Sessions in SAP Learning Hub: Interactive Learning with SAP
entry.content:
What are Live Sessions in  SAP Learning Hub? Live Sessions are live virtual events, typically lasting one to two hours, led by SAP experts. They take place throughout the year and cover a wide range of SAP topics. In 2024, more than 200,000 learners took advantage of this offering. To reach as many participants worldwide as possible, the sessions are offered in two time zones and in English.

Live Sessions come in various formats. The majority are:

  • Expert Deep Dives into specific SAP topics.

In addition, there are:

  • What’s New Sessions,
  • Stay Certified Sessions, and
  • Get Started Sessions.

Advantages of Live Sessions in  SAP Learning Hub Live Sessions offer a wide range of benefits:

  1. Expert Knowledge: Up-to-date expert knowledge is shared by SAP professionals, presenting the latest topics and technologies. This ensures that participants are always informed about the latest developments and products.
  2. Interactivity: Participants can ask questions in real time and engage in discussions. This not only promotes understanding of complex topics but also allows for the exchange of experiences and best practices. Learners can also benefit from the questions posed by others.
  3. Practical Relevance: The sessions often focus on real-life use cases. This helps participants apply what they’ve learned directly to their work.
  4. Networking: Participants have the opportunity to network with like-minded individuals from the SAP community. This encourages knowledge sharing and can lead to valuable professional connections.
  5. Flexibility: The sessions are offered at various times to accommodate different time zones and work hours. Some sessions are also recorded
  6. Cost-Effectiveness: For subscribers of SAP Learning Hub, Live Sessions are included in the subscription, providing a cost-effective way to continue learning.
  7. Relevance of Topics: Live session topics reflect the latest trends and developments in the SAP world. Recently, there have been sessions on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Analytics Cloud, integration of various SAP solutions, and many sessions on Artificial Intelligence.

Live Sessions in SAP Learning Hub complement the on-demand, self-paced learning content perfectly and offer SAP professionals the opportunity to continuously expand their knowledge and stay up-to-date. They are an important building block for professional development in the rapidly changing world of SAP technologies.

 

For those interested, it is recommended to regularly check the offerings in SAP Learning Hub and incorporate relevant Live Sessions into their personal learning plan. Selection filters, such as level (prior knowledge) or role of the learner, help when choosing relevant Live Sessions.
The combination of flexible online learning and interactive live events makes the SAP Learning Hub a comprehensive resource for anyone who wants to stay up-to-date in the SAP world.

Live Sessions are published on learning.sap.com and the schedule is continuously expanded to cover all current product areas. You can find upcoming Live Sessions here.
For regular updates on SAP’s learning offerings, we recommend subscribing to the SAP Training Newsletter.

We look forward to your questions and comments.

entry.title: How Data driven Change Management with Deep Transformation Insights ensures successful SAP Business
entry.content:
This blog highlights the critical role of Organizational Change Management (OCM) in the successful implementation of SAP S/4HANA. It delves into how SAP's Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) service leverages data-driven insights to help organizations manage change effectively, ensuring employee acceptance and engagement throughout the transformation process.

The Challenge of SAP S/4HANA Implementations: A Change Management Perspective

Implementing SAP S/4HANA is a transformative process that goes beyond a mere technological upgrade. It reshapes business operations, IT systems, and employee behavior while embracing a Fit-to-Standard mindset This increased focus on standards helps to reduce the time and costs associated with implementation and support, and also drives business efficiencies as well as scalability. It is SAP’s recommended approach to enable faster adoption of SAP S/4HANA.

Despite the recognized importance of Organizational Change Management (OCM) in ensuring successful business transformation, many organizations struggle to integrate it effectively into their strategy. We recently did a Change Assessment with more than 800 respondents on the perception of different important OCM related dimensions. See here some highlights:

Key Challenges in OCM Implementations

Strategic Alignment and Ownership

  • Confusion about who should take ownership of OCM within the organization
  • Lack of clarity in defining desired business outcomes
  • Misconceptions about OCM's scope and responsibilities

Leadership Involvement

  • 64% of companies acknowledge senior leaders' crucial role in driving change
  • Only 56% report active engagement of leaders with their teams
  • Lack of visible support undermines morale and alignment

Communication and Change Story

  • Only 48% of organizations actively communicate SAP program updates
  • 54% have a clearly defined Change Story

Resistance to change evolves when discussions focus around Fit-Gap keeping the current business processes in the future system and NOT focusing on fit-to-standard.

Past Experiences and Skepticism

  • Poor outcomes in previous OCM efforts lead to skepticism
  • Some organizations exclude OCM from new projects due to past experiences

Resource Constraints and ROI Measurement

  • Lack of internal structures and expertise for effective OCM implementation
  • 71% believe their Change Management Strategy explains the SAP program's importance
  • Only 47% feel end users are equipped to apply SAP solutions in daily work
  • OCM is often viewed as an optional expense rather than a critical success factor
  • Difficulty in justifying additional spending on OCM
  • Only 44% of organizations actively assess the effectiveness of their change initiatives
  • Lack of clear metrics to demonstrate ROI

Overcoming these challenges requires a holistic approach to OCM, emphasizing clear ownership, leadership involvement, effective communication, and measurable outcomes. By addressing these key areas, organizations can maximize the benefits of their SAP S/4HANA implementation and ensure a smoother transition for all stakeholders.

To address such challenges the Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) service from SAP comes into play. With this data driven approach, organizations can safeguard the success of their SAP S/4HANA implementations. It's not just about deploying new technology; it's about preparing the people who will use it, ensuring sustainable adoption, and unlocking the full potential of the transformation.

DTI is built around five key dimensions or sentiments—Awareness, Acceptance, Enablement, Empowerment, and Commitment—that provide a structured path for guiding individuals through change, fostering both readiness and long-term engagement. Let’s look at these briefly.

Research-based Change Sentiments to Accelerate Change and Adoption in SAP Implementations

Based on research conducted by organizational scientists like A. Armenakis, this five important factors have been identified as they determine the degree of employee buy-in by organizational change recipients.

Awareness

  • Foundation of successful transformation
  • Ensures recognition of the need for change
  • Focuses on communicating the "why" behind the change
  • Aligns change initiative with broader business goals

Acceptance

  • Builds on awareness
  • Addresses emotional and intellectual readiness
  • Focuses on trust-building and reducing resistance
  • Creates psychological safety for employees

Enablement

  • Bridge between readiness and action
  • Equips individuals with necessary tools, training, and resources
  • Creates a supportive environment for adapting to new roles
  • Minimizes disruption and maximizes productivity during transformation

Empowerment

  • Fosters ownership and confidence among employees
  • Grants authority for decision-making within respective roles
  • Cultivates a culture of distributed decision-making
  • Inspires innovation and accountability at all levels

Adoption and Commitment

  • Emphasizes alignment between personal and organizational goals
  • Prioritizes intrinsic and extrinsic motivation
  • Focuses on recognition and reinforcement
  • Ensures continued engagement, real productive usage of the new processes or tools

 

From an SAP perspective, we assembled these factors and change sentiments into a framework aligned with the SAP Activate methodology and focused on SAP systems implementations.

We call this framework Deep Transformation Insights (DTI), utilizing change sentiment questionnaires throughout the implementation cycle for customers. DTI contains assessment tools that can be administered at any stage of the implementation process.

The insights obtained can serve as:

(a) An indicator of the degree of adoption/acceptance among users

(b) An assessment of issues in project enablement that can adversely impact the success of the implementation

(c) A basis for planning and executing learning to enhance user adoption

(d) A regular data driven pulse-check to monitor success of actions to improve the five dimensions.

Customer Case highlighting Activities from Data-Driven Insights to Accelerate User Adoption in SAP S/4HANA Projects

So how could this look like in SAP projects? To make it tangible we would like to share a real world customer case.   

The customer prepared for a planned implementation of SAP S/4HANA supporting an enterprise-wide digital transformation strategy to enhance scalability and efficiency. Further business alignment goals included greater simplicity, efficiency, insight, collaboration, transparency, and compliance, among others.

The company achieved success by prioritizing employee buy-in and minimizing disruption. Using SAP's Deep Transformation Insights service, they enhanced their change management with digital tools and data-driven insights. This people-centric approach effectively managed the transformation's complexity, leading to a successful implementation After implementing the DTI service, the company established and orchestrated an employee engagement feedback architecture using a structured change-sentiment framework across project and organizational units.
Additionally, they gained better insights into stakeholder perceptions and experiences throughout the project lifecycle. They now use these insights as the foundation to accelerate and support solution adoption.

"Utilizing DTI was seamless, and the insights we gained from the team were very thorough, thoughtful, and actionable. We were able to easily see that the work we were doing was having the intended effect because of the results from DTI"
Director Organizational Change Management, Kyndryl

Summary

Organizational Change Management (OCM) is crucial for ensuring successful adoption and realizing the full potential of any digital transformation. However, many organizations face challenges in integrating OCM into their implementation strategy.

Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) offers a comprehensive solution to these challenges, providing a data-driven approach to change management that aligns with the SAP Activate methodology. By focusing on five proven key sentiments—Awareness, Acceptance, Enablement, Empowerment, and Commitment—DTI helps organizations guide their employees through the change process, fostering engagement and long-term success. Find out more about SAP Organizational Change Management on sap.com, or contact us per e-mail.

We look forward to discuss your questions in the comments.

entry.title: Adaptive Transformation - Ein innovativer Ansatz für komplexe Veränderungsprojekte
entry.content:
In der jüngsten SAP Change und Training Kaffee Ecke folgten wir der Einladung von Eike Wagner auf seine Change Hut – seiner Skihütte im Zillertal, auf der Interessierte jederzeit zu einem Austausch über Change und Transformation willkommen sind.

Eike präsentierte das Konzept der adaptiven Transformation. Der Fokus lag auf modernen Ansätzen im Change Management, insbesondere im Kontext komplexer SAP-Implementierungen und Organisationsveränderungen.

Anfangs verwies er auf klassische Modelle wie Lewin (1947), Kübler-Ross (1969) und Kotter (1995) und erläuterte, warum diese in der heutigen dynamischen Geschäftswelt oft an ihre Grenzen stoßen. Auch neuere Konzepte wie Agilität, Co-Creation und New Work sind seiner Ansicht nach keine Allheilmittel, besonders wenn sie oberflächlich implementiert werden.

Zudem verändern technologische Möglichkeiten, besonders künstliche Intelligenz, die Funktionsweise von Organisationen grundlegend. Eike argumentierte, dass traditionelle Change-Modelle mit dieser Entwicklung oft nicht Schritt halten können.

 

Truly Adaptive Transformation

Als Denkanstoß stellte Eike das Konzept der "Truly Adaptive Transformation" vor. Dieses Modell geht über Agilität hinaus und berücksichtigt psychologische, soziologische und umweltbezogene Faktoren, die alle miteinander vernetzt sind. Diese 3 Themenbereiche beeinflussen unser Verhalten. Ziel ist es, viele Menschen schnell und umfassend zu Verhaltensänderungen zu bewegen. Dafür ist es nötig, die Determinanten hinter diesen Treibern zu identifizieren und deren Abhängigkeiten.

 

 

Eike betonte, dass jede organisationale Transformation im Kern bedeutet, dass viele Menschen so schnell und so umfassend wie möglich ihr Verhalten ändern müssen. Das Vorgehen der Truly adaptive Transformation ist laut Eike komplex, aber nicht kompliziert.

Eike ging in diesem Kontext auf die Unterscheidung zwischen komplexen und komplizierten Herausforderungen ein:  Er veranschaulichte dies am Beispiel eines Flugzeugtriebwerks (kompliziert, aber planbar und alle Faktoren sind bekannt) im Vergleich zu Mayonnaise (komplex, nicht rückgängig zu machen). Diese Differenzierung ist entscheidend für die Wahl des richtigen Vorgehensmodells in Transformationsprojekten.

Truly adaptive Transformation beginnt mit der Definition einer klaren Vision. Dabei gilt es  z.B. zu klären, was man mit der Einführung einer bestimmten Software wirklich erreichen möchte. Weiterhin ist die Identifikation notwendiger Technologien und Veränderungsbereiche nötig. Eike betonte die Wichtigkeit der Abhängigkeiten (kausal, zeitlich) der einzelnen Themen untereinander.

Das Vorgehen beinhaltet, dass autonome Teams für verschiedene Themenbereiche gebildet werden, die parallel mit unterschiedlichen Methoden arbeiten können. Eine regelmäßige übergreifende Synchronisation alle 2-3 Monate stellt die Kohärenz sicher. Dabei ist es nicht relevant, dass die Teams alle agil arbeiten. Die Vorgehensweise (agil oder nach dem linearen Wasserfallmodell) der einzelnen Teams hängt vom Thema und der Sinnhaftigkeit ab. Die Frage ist: Welche Arbeitsweise hilft dem Team, um das Ergebnis, den Auftrag, den sie haben, gut zu erreichen. Manchmal ist es agil und manchmal ist es eben nicht agil, unabhängig davon, was sich die Teams wünschen.

Auf der obigen Folie ist das Vorgehen nochmals illustriert.

Eikes Empfehlung war, alle relevanten Faktoren einer Transformation zu sammeln: Neben der Technologie, Tools / Systeme (bei einer Technologie-Implementierung gesetzt), hilfreiche / notwendige Strukturveränderung,  Prozesse, die im System abgebildet werden sollen, und Prozesse, die an das System anzupassen sind sowie Mindset Skills Wissen, Knowledge der Mitarbeitenden.

Dabei betonte Eike die Wichtigkeit der echten Einbindung der Teilnehmenden.

Er zeigte eine toolbasierte Visualisierung von OKRs (Objectives & Key Results) auf Jahresebene und auf Quartalssebene, wobei Abhängigkeiten zwischen den Jahreszielen und den Quartalszielen dargestellt wurden. Basierend darauf kann in Abhängigkeit von den Schwerpunkten, die sich Teams setzen direkt visuell festgestellt werden, ob zu viele Abhängigkeiten in den parallelen Teams vorhanden sind und damit die Wahrscheinlichkeit erhöht ist, zu scheitern. Damit wird deutlich, dass entweder die Ziele angepasst werden müssen oder, wenn das nicht möglich / gewünscht ist, die Komplexität bewusst in Kauf zu nehmen und dafür die Abstimmungen in kürzeren Abständen zu halten.

 

Eike stellte danach das Konzept des GuP (Gestaltungs- und Umsetzungspaket, im Original Design and Implementation Package, DIP) vor. Dieses erlaubt die parallele Gestaltung und Umsetzung verschiedener Aspekte der Transformation. Er betonte die Wichtigkeit, frühzeitig über organisatorische Anpassungen nachzudenken, selbst wenn die finale Softwarekonfiguration bei einer IT-Transformation noch nicht feststeht.

Eine wichtige Rolle in Eikes Modell spielt der Chief Transformation Designer. Diese Position ist verantwortlich für die Vernetzung der Teams und schützt vor übermäßiger Beeinflussung durch das Top-Managements. Eike unterstrich die Bedeutung dieser Rolle für den Erfolg von Transformationsprojekten.

Diskussion

In der Diskussion wurden verschiedene Aspekte in der Praxis beleuchtet. Eine Teilnehmerin fragte nach Eikes Einschätzung zu den Modellen von Holt (Change Readiness) und Venkatesh (UTAUT-Modell), die den Change begünstigen sollen, und sprach die Notwendigkeit der Veränderung, soziale Unterstützung, Selbstwirksamkeit usw. an. Eikes Einschätzung differenzierte zwischen individueller und organisationaler Veränderungsbereitschaft. Sollte in der Organisation die Veränderungsbereitschaft fehlen, könne man versuchen, diese zu erhöhen oder die Veränderung zu starten und mit Change- und Trainingsinterventionen zu begleiten. Eike empfiehlt dabei, ins Tun zu kommen - etwas machen und verproben, inwieweit die Bereitschaft wirklich gering ist wie angenommen oder nicht, und daran anknüpfend den nächsten Schritt zu planen.

Eike teilte auch Erfahrungen aus realen Projekten, wie eine SAP-Implementierung bei BMW vor über 20 Jahren. Er beschrieb, wie ein Rollenspiel des Rekrutierungsprozesses mit dem Angeln hinter einer Stellwand zu einem Aha-Moment bei den Beteiligten führte und die Notwendigkeit für Veränderung verdeutlichte.

Ein weiterer Diskussionspunkt war die Integration mit bestehenden Methoden wie SAFe oder LeSS. Eike erklärte, dass sein Modell Elemente dieser Frameworks integrieren könne, aber flexibler in der Anpassung an spezifische Projektanforderungen sei.

Bei SAP Beratern gilt es beispielsweise auch, sich an die Methodik des Kunden anzupassen. Nutzt dieser SAFe, sollten man auch danach arbeiten, auch wenn man aus verschiedenen Gründen das jeweilige Modell nicht präferiert.

Zudem gab es eine längere Diskussion zu den Ansätzen der Agilität vs. der linearen Methodik, auch Wasserfall genannt. So meinte zum Beispiel Torben Mauch, der übrigens auch auf der Change Hut anwesend war, dass manchmal Wasserfall Modelle durchaus Sinn machen und man dies nicht ideologisch sehen sollte.

Abschließend betonte Eike, dass die zunehmende Vielfalt an Möglichkeiten und die Geschwindigkeit von Veränderungen flexiblere Ansätze im Change Management erfordern. Er ermutigte die Teilnehmer, bewusst über ihr Vorgehen bei Transformationsprojekten nachzudenken und die Methode basierend auf der spezifischen Situation und Komplexität des Projekts zu wählen.

Die Session endete mit einem Ausblick auf die kommenden Kaffeecken:

Global vernetzt: Change Kommunikation für eine weltweite SAP S/4HANA Transformation am 20.03.: https://events.sap.com/de/sap-change-training/de/home

und

SAP PoC - Der Einsatz von KI bei der Erstellung von Trainingsbedarfsanalysen und Lerninhalten am 24.04. https://go4.events.sap.com/eur-kaffee-ecke-april/de_de/home.html

SAVE the date - Das jährliche Community Treffen am 25.6. wird demnächst zur Registrierung geöffnet.

Wer sich über Eikes Arbeit informieren will, findet hier weitere Infos

Eikes Website https://changeworkout.rocks

Eikes Change Community https://changeworkout.community

entry.title: Co-working with AI: Transforming the Modern Workplace
entry.content:
“Today, as AI systems still mature, our challenge is not implementing AI, but redesigning jobs, and business processes around AI. And that’s why success with AI is a people problem, not a technology one.” Josh Bersin

AI is transforming the workplace in fundamental ways, requiring organizations to rethink how people work rather than just focusing on implementing new technology. Success requires addressing four key areas:

  • Redefining jobs and skills for an AI-enhanced future,
  • Equipping managers to lead hybrid human-AI teams effectively,
  • Fostering productive team collaboration with AI, and
  • Helping individuals maintain their unique value while working alongside AI.

The goal isn't to replace humans with AI, but to create partnerships that combine human creativity and judgment with AI capabilities to achieve better outcomes than either could alone. Organizations need clear roadmaps for this transformation that address both practical implementation and human concerns about the future of work.

Target audience: Managers/Business leaders, HR/organizational development professionals, and knowledge workers.

pdf version of the article for download 

AI integration in the workplace

Chapter 1: The Future of Work in the AI Era

The workplace is changing in fundamental ways as organizations move from traditional ways of working to AI-enhanced operations. This chapter looks at how job roles are evolving, how expertise is becoming more accessible to everyone, and how organizations are tackling the tricky question of what work should look like when AI is part of the team. These changes touch everything from individual jobs to how organizations are structured, and we need to think carefully about how to keep both efficiency and meaningful human contribution in the mix.

Future of Work in the AI Era

Transforming Professional Roles

The workplace is evolving as AI systems grow from simple tools to smart partners that can think and learn. Organizations are getting creative about combining human expertise with AI capabilities, focusing on achieving meaningful results rather than just automating tasks.

We’ll be seeing professionals shift from routine task work to more strategic roles that focus on critical thinking and decision-making. For example, content creators will become strategic curators and facilitators of dynamic and intelligent systems, while the role of developers will change from manual coders to orchestrators of AI-driven development ecosystems. This shift will create many new jobs but also requires new cognitive skills.

We're also seeing new hybrid roles pop up that blend technical AI know-how with deep subject expertise and people skills. These roles are crucial for striking the right balance between innovation, risk management, and ethical considerations as we bring AI into our work.

Getting AI implementation right means taking a comprehensive look at how we work. This isn't just about adding new tools—it's about rethinking our business processes, workflows, skills, and jobs. Josh Bersin's four-stage AI transformation model provides a helpful roadmap: Start with AI assistance (same job, better tools), move to augmentation (same job, tools eliminate work), then to work replacement (new job, redesigned process, agents automate work), and finally to autonomy (new job, redesigned process, people "manage" AI agents).

Think About This

  • How does bringing AI into the workplace challenge traditional career paths, and what new doors does it open?
  • What can help employees maintain a sense of purpose when AI is reshaping their roles?
  • What tasks should stay human-driven, and why?

 

The Vision Gap in AI Integration

Here's a big problem we're seeing in current AI transformation efforts: organizations aren't painting a clear picture of what the future looks like. While companies are quick to announce AI initiatives and talk up efficiency gains, few are really explaining how their organizations will work when advanced AI systems and autonomous agents are fully part of the team. Vague promises about better efficiency and productivity don't answer the real questions people have about how their jobs will evolve, what skills they'll need, and what their career paths might look like.

Leading organizations need to move beyond abstract promises and provide detailed roadmaps for the future. These roadmaps should spell out how current roles will evolve and what new positions might emerge as AI becomes more integrated. They need to show how human judgment and creativity will work alongside AI capabilities, creating a clear picture of how human and machine intelligence can complement each other. Organizations also need to map out career progression paths in this AI-enhanced landscape, helping employees understand how their professional journeys might unfold.

This vision gap isn't just about communication—it shows that organizations need to think more deeply about how they'll evolve alongside advancing AI capabilities. Only through careful planning can organizations provide the clarity and transparency their employees need and deserve.

Think About This

  • How does your organization plan to balance getting more efficient while keeping work meaningful for employees?
  • What ways do employees have to share feedback or raise concerns about AI's role in workplace changes?

 

Managing Emotions and Team Dynamics

When we bring AI in as a sophisticated workplace partner, it creates some interesting psychological and social challenges that go beyond just learning new tech. These changes affect how people feel about their work and how teams interact in fundamental ways.

One tricky thing is that people tend to think of AI tools as being more human-like than they really are. Team members might unconsciously attribute human characteristics to these systems, which can lead to either expecting too much from AI or being overly skeptical about what it can do.

Another challenge is that AI can generate content really quickly, which creates new cognitive demands for team members. Workers need to develop skills in quickly filtering through information, evaluating it, and refining it. This creates an interesting tension between AI's speed and our human need for thoughtful analysis (is what the AI produced relevant, correct, and trustworthy?) and careful decision-making.

Organizations need both structural and cultural strategies to handle these dynamics effectively. On the structural side, successful implementation includes clear frameworks for how to work with AI, regular check-ins to review progress, and clear accountability measures. On the cultural side, high-performing teams create an environment that values both what AI can do and what humans bring to the table, encouraging people to actively evaluate and validate AI suggestions rather than just accepting them without question.

Think About This

  • What frameworks can help us balance AI's quick output with thoughtful human analysis?
  • How does treating AI as human-like affect how teams work together and make decisions?
  • What characteristics should we build into AI personas to make them most effective?

 

Democratizing Expertise

AI is making specialized knowledge more accessible throughout organizations. With user-friendly AI interfaces, more employees can now access knowledge and handle tasks that used to require special training. This levels the playing field and changes how we measure and value work. Teams can now produce higher quality work, with AI tools helping to standardize basic capabilities and reduce performance variations.

This shift brings both opportunities and challenges in redefining how we value work. While we expect overall work quality to go up and skill differences between team members to decrease, there's a growing need for individuals to find ways to stand out and add unique value. Teams and individuals need to get creative about how they can distinguish themselves in a workplace where AI is enhancing everyone's capabilities.

But there are some important risks to watch out for when employees use AI without enough background knowledge to properly evaluate the results e.g.

  • People might misunderstand or misinterpret what AI produces, which can lead to poor decisions.
  • They might not be able to effectively judge whether AI-generated content is relevant, accurate, or appropriate.
  • There might be unintended ethical issues, like privacy violations or unfair decision-making processes.

Think About This

  • How does making specialized knowledge more accessible change traditional ideas about expertise and authority?
  • How can we prevent AI from making everyone's work look the same?

 

The Value of Work in an AI-Enhanced Environment

As AI makes expertise more accessible to everyone, we need to think differently about how we define and measure the value of work. This means coming up with smarter ways to evaluate performance and compensate people—ways that look at both individual excellence and how well people work with AI.

These frameworks should focus on results that show people are using AI effectively while maintaining human oversight and creativity. We might look at things like how innovatively people use AI, how well they collaborate with AI, and their ability to drive meaningful improvements through this partnership.

This change goes beyond just evaluating performance—it affects how we incentivize success and compensate work. Traditional productivity-based models don't quite fit this new work environment. Organizations should develop ways to recognize and reward effective human-AI teamwork, celebrating individuals and teams that excel at leveraging AI to enhance their work.

When we connect rewards to concrete outcomes of human-AI collaboration—like improved customer satisfaction, cost savings, or market growth—we make sure individual goals line up with what the organization wants to achieve. This approach motivates employees and teams to embrace AI as a collaborative tool while reinforcing that work value comes from impact rather than just getting through a lot of tasks.

Think About This

  • How can organizations stay transparent about evaluating contributions when tasks are shared between humans and AI?
  • How can organizations ensure everyone has fair access to AI tools to prevent creating a digital divide at work?

 

Chapter 2: Leading Teams in the Age of AI

AI in the workplace presents managers with some unique challenges and opportunities in team leadership. This chapter looks at the crucial role managers play in successfully bringing AI into their teams while keeping team spirit and effectiveness strong. We'll explore how to encourage ethical AI use, build trust, orchestrate human-AI collaboration, and foster team growth. This shows us how management practices need to evolve an AI-first approach that effectively guides teams through the complexities of human-AI collaboration.

Leading Teams in the Age of AI

Encouraging Ethical and Responsible AI Use

Managers play a key role in making sure their teams use AI responsibly and ethically. They need to champion ethical AI practices, raise awareness about data privacy, and help their teams follow organizational guidelines.

Having open discussions about what AI can and can't do, and what that means for the team, is essential for building a culture of transparency and shared responsibility. Managers should create spaces where team members feel comfortable voicing concerns, suggesting improvements, and working together to tackle ethical challenges. This approach builds trust and encourages people to engage proactively with AI tools.

Comprehensive training programs focused on ethical AI practices, security, and data privacy help strengthen responsible use. Managers can support their teams by developing clear, practical guidelines for AI workflows and setting measurable standards for accountability in AI applications.

When it comes to using AI and making AI-driven decisions, managers need to walk the talk—they should actively demonstrate ethical AI practices through their daily actions and leadership approach. This means being clear about how AI tools are being used in team processes and decision-making, emphasizing that humans are still keeping an eye on things, and consistently putting integrity and fairness first in all AI-related work. When managers actively spot risks and promote fairness in AI-driven decisions at the team level, it really helps build responsible AI use.

Think About This

  • How well do I understand the potential ethical risks, like data privacy concerns or biases, that come with the AI tools my team and I are using?
  • What specific actions am I taking to encourage open discussions about what AI means for my team?

 

Building Trust in AI-Enhanced Teams

Bringing AI into the workplace is a significant change that requires thoughtful leadership and change management. Team members often have valid worries about job security, changes to their jobs and workflows, and understanding how decisions are made. The success of AI integration depends heavily on how managers address the human element and build trust throughout the process.

Here are three key strategies that support effective AI integration:

  • First, establish psychological safety by clearly communicating practical information about what AI tools are capable of and their limitations. Explain specifically how AI fits into existing workflows and where human judgment remains essential.
  • Second, get the team involved in decisions about AI adoption. Successful managers actively include team members in choosing tools and planning implementation. They organize hands-on demos, gather feedback about potential AI solutions, and adjust their plans based on what the team says.
  • Third, position AI as a collaborative partner for both routine and creative work. AI can both handle repetitive tasks and enhance creative and strategic activities by generating new ideas and supporting complex problem-solving. This approach helps teams see AI as a tool that amplifies what humans can do rather than replacing them.

Think About This

  • How well do I understand my team members' specific concerns about AI adoption? What signals am I picking up during team discussions about AI?
  • In what ways could AI enhance rather than replace what makes each team member unique?
  • How ready is my team to work with AI?

 

Orchestrating Human-AI Collaboration

Orchestrating human-AI collaboration involves more than just adopting new tools—it means reimagining roles, workflows, and team culture. Success depends on taking a nuanced, human-centered approach to all these elements.

Managers need to foster a mindset that sees AI as something that enhances rather than replaces human effort. This cultural shift means embedding AI collaboration into team values and practices while keeping the spotlight on human expertise.

When we bring AI into the mix, teams can redefine their roles by combining human expertise with AI capabilities. Managers should guide their teams in using their unique skills strategically while giving appropriate tasks to AI systems. Getting teams involved in planning and implementing new workflows helps ensure smooth transitions and gets everyone on board. This approach encourages experimenting with innovative ways of working while requiring new ways to measure success that emphasize strategic contributions over routine outputs.

To make integration successful, managers need to regularly monitor how AI affects team dynamics and individual roles, assess whether things are staying aligned with team values and goals, and adjust collaboration strategies based on feedback and performance data.

Think About This

  • How can I actively involve my team in figuring out which tasks should stay human-driven versus those that can be handed off to AI, making sure we're both efficient and keeping team satisfaction high?
  • What processes can I put in place to regularly check how AI is affecting team dynamics and roles, and how can I make sure we act on feedback to optimize collaboration?

 

Fostering Team Growth and Adaptability

As AI keeps advancing rapidly, teams need to stay nimble and embrace continuous learning. Managers should model openness to AI-driven changes and actively explore ways new technologies can improve workflows. Supporting the development of relevant skills, like prompt engineering and interpreting AI outputs, helps ensure team members can use AI effectively in their roles.

Comprehensive training programs tailored to specific roles and responsibilities help build both confidence and competence with AI technologies. These programs should give hands-on experience with AI tools and platforms, letting people practice using them in their daily tasks. An example is the Promptathon, where participants use generative AI to address real-world challenges and improve their prompt-writing skills.

Having a growth mindset is essential for long-term success in the AI era. Managers need to create environments where curiosity and experimentation are valued, and continuous learning is a priority. When we recognize adaptability, innovative thinking, and effective AI solution implementation, it reinforces these behaviors within teams.

Cross-functional collaboration and knowledge sharing help maximize the benefits of AI adoption. Setting up regular forums for sharing experiences, best practices, and lessons learned speeds up collective learning and ensures that diverse perspectives shape AI implementation strategies.

Think About This

  • How can I show openness to AI-driven changes and demonstrate how these technologies can enhance workflows and team effectiveness?
  • How can I create a culture that values curiosity, experimentation, and adaptability, and make sure team members feel safe to innovate and take risks with AI-driven solutions?

 

Chapter 3: Teamwork Redefined

When AI joins your team, it can fundamentally change how everyone works together. This chapter digs into how the role of AI evolves and how to balance tasks between humans and AI. We'll explore the importance of building team cohesion and fostering transparency about AI use so that teams can maintain their collaborative spirit while making the most of AI capabilities.

Teamwork Redefined

The Evolving Role of AI in Teams

Modern teams work in what we call hybrid intelligence environments – spaces where human and artificial intelligence come together to create something better than either could achieve alone. AI has grown from being a basic helper to becoming more like a virtual team member and expert consultant. To make this work well, teams need to regularly check how they're using AI to ensure it aligns with their goals, values, and culture.

Ethan Mollick's "cyborg" and "centaur" model offers a valuable perspective on human-AI collaboration: The "cyborg" approach is about seamlessly blending human and AI capabilities to keep improving outcomes. The "centaur" approach, on the other hand, suggests strategically dividing tasks – letting AI handle the analytical heavy lifting while humans focus on creativity and judgment. Both ways aim to enhance rather than replace human capabilities.

We're now seeing AI agents that can act as virtual team members with their own roles. These agents can tackle complex problems, manage sophisticated workflows, and even collaborate with other AI agents on challenging tasks. But bringing these advanced AI teammates on board requires careful consideration of team dynamics, clear communication guidelines, and attention to both performance and job satisfaction.

Think About This

  • How can we develop a shared understanding of what AI can and can't do, including ethical considerations, to build trust in our collaboration?
  • In what ways can AI's capabilities complement, rather than replace, what makes our team special?

 

Finding the Right Balance

When teams start working with AI, they are facing an important question: should they fine-tune existing processes or completely reimagine how they work? Making this decision requires understanding what AI can do, what the team wants to achieve, what skills team members have, and how roles might change. Success comes from finding the sweet spot between getting more efficient and keeping the team working well together.

Start by taking a good look at your tasks and workflows. Identify areas that need improvement and decide whether to gradually bring in AI or go for a bigger change. Map out how human and AI tasks will connect, establish clear handoff points, and optimize how everyone works together.

As AI systems evolve from basic helpers to genuine intellectual partners, they can significantly boost productivity and quality. This evolution lets teams unlock new capabilities and tackle bigger challenges, but organizations need to think carefully about how much AI to integrate based on their goals and resources. These decisions directly influence how AI complements human efforts and reshapes team roles.

To manage these transitions well, keep an eye on how communication patterns, team dynamics, and motivation levels change as roles adapt. Make sure that improvements in efficiency don't come at the cost of quality. Put safeguards in place to prevent over-reliance on AI, maintain human oversight for critical decisions, and prevent skill erosion from too much automation. When you distribute tasks fairly between humans and AI, you help your team stay resilient and adaptable.

To keep improving, measure how well humans and AI work together and set up feedback systems that drive progress. These measures help maintain the right balance between speed, accuracy, and adaptability as workflows evolve.

Think About This

  • How do we decide which tasks should stay human-led versus those we can delegate to AI?
  • How can we ensure AI helps our team innovate and collaborate better without disrupting trust and team spirit?

 

Building Team Cohesion

 When bringing AI into your team, keeping everyone connected and working well together becomes even more important than usual. Strong team bonds built on trust and open communication make it easier for everyone to be transparent about how they're using AI, and when people are open about their AI work, it helps build stronger team relationships. This creates a positive cycle that keeps getting better over time.

Building team cohesion in an AI-enhanced workplace isn't something that just happens on its own—it needs a thoughtful approach. Start with setting up clear ways for everyone to communicate. Regular team meetings focused on how AI implementation is going can help, along with creating specific channels where people can raise concerns or suggest improvements. When you have structured ways for people to give feedback about AI-related issues, problems get spotted and fixed faster. It also helps to keep detailed records of how AI makes decisions, which keeps everything transparent and makes it clear who's responsible for what.

For teams to work well together, everyone needs to be on the same page about AI. Regular training sessions provide team members with a clear understanding of the capabilities and limitations of AI, equipping them with the necessary technical knowledge to work with confidence. Having standard guidelines for how to use and oversee AI tools keeps everyone working consistently, while clearly defining who does what in human-AI collaboration prevents confusion and overlap. Keeping the team updated about changes in AI systems helps everyone stay aligned with how the technology is developing.

Teams that handle these aspects well often find that AI actually helps bring people closer together rather than pushing them apart. When everyone understands their role in working with AI and feels comfortable sharing their experiences—both good and bad—it creates a stronger, more resilient team that can tackle bigger challenges together.

Think About This

  • How can we create an environment where team members feel safe questioning AI outputs and sharing their concerns?
  • What challenges might come up in keeping the team connected as AI becomes a bigger part of our work, and how can we prepare for them?

 

Fostering Transparency

To build trust with AI in your organization, start with transparency. When teams feel comfortable sharing both their successes and setbacks with AI tools, everyone learns and grows together. Being clear about who's doing what – whether it's a person or AI – helps maintain accountability while acknowledging how AI supports our work. By sharing regular updates about how teams are using AI and what's working best, we can help everyone get the most out of these tools while building trust along the way.

To make sure everyone can contribute meaningfully in an AI-enhanced workplace, we need to level the playing field. This means making sure everyone has equal access to AI tools and training, and providing extra support through mentoring programs for those who need it. When we bring diverse perspectives into decisions about how to use AI, we create solutions that work better for everyone.

As AI becomes a bigger part of our workday, keeping strong human connections is more important than ever. Regular team activities help build those vital social bonds, while taking time to celebrate both individual and team achievements keeps the group united. Making sure teams have plenty of opportunities to work together face-to-face helps maintain those essential human connections.

Reflective Questions

  • How can your team create an environment where people feel comfortable sharing their experiences with AI – both good and bad?
  • What kinds of peer learning and mentoring could help your colleagues get better at using AI tools?

 

Chapter 4: Thriving Individually in an AI-Enhanced Workplace

The changes AI brings to the workplace affect everyone differently, and each of us needs to figure out how to adapt and grow in our own way. This chapter looks at how individuals can keep their professional identity strong while developing new skills for working with AI. We'll explore how to develop hybrid capabilities that blend human and AI strengths, transform our sense of professional purpose, and build confidence in working with AI tools. These insights will help you navigate your evolving role while staying in control of your career path.

Thriving Individually in an AI-Enhanced Workplace

Leveraging Hybrid Intelligence

When we talk about hybrid intelligence, we're really talking about finding ways for humans and AI to work together that make both better. The key to making this partnership work is being clear about who does what and making sure humans stay in control of their roles while deciding how much they want to work with AI. When we get this balance right, AI becomes a powerful tool that helps us do our jobs better without taking over our expertise or decision-making.

To make the most of hybrid intelligence, you need a solid grasp of how AI works. At the same time, it's crucial to keep developing those uniquely human skills like contextual thinking and ethical judgment. These abilities help us use AI wisely while keeping the final say in our hands.

Hybrid intelligence is changing how we tackle our daily tasks, turning them into collaborative efforts between humans and machines. To make this collaboration work, we need to think of AI not just as another tool but as a teammate with its own strengths and limitations.

Think About This

  • What ethical considerations come up when AI systems start making decisions that humans traditionally handle?
  • How do you decide which tasks to hand off to AI and which ones to keep under human control?

 

Cultivating Dual Expertise

Working in an AI-enhanced workplace means you need to be good at two things: your main job and understanding how to work with AI. Having this dual expertise helps you use AI effectively while keeping your professional skills sharp and relevant. But finding this balance isn't always easy.

As we develop confidence in AI and start letting it handle more of our tasks, we might accidentally let our own expertise and critical thinking skills get rusty. This growing dependence on AI can reduce direct cognitive engagement, making it harder for us to spot when something's wrong with AI outputs. We might fall into what is called "automation complacency"—where we trust AI too much and stop questioning its decisions.

As AI systems get more sophisticated, it takes more expertise to properly evaluate their outputs. That's why it's crucial to stay deeply engaged with your field—we need that knowledge to spot when AI might be giving inaccurate or biased results. Building this dual expertise means committing to lifelong learning, constantly improving both our main professional skills and our AI literacy.

Another challenge is understanding how AI makes decisions. When AI systems are a "black box" where we can't see how decisions are made, it raises concerns about transparency and trust. This lack of clarity can make it harder for professionals to feel confident about their work and for organizations to maintain accountability.

Think About This

  • How can you balance your time between staying sharp in your field and learning about AI?
  • What role can you play in helping bridge the gap between AI experts and domain experts in your organization?

 

Transforming Professional Purpose

When AI becomes part of the workplace, it can really shake up how we think about expertise and what it means to be good at our jobs. As AI systems take over more routine tasks and even start handling some creative work, we need to rethink what makes us valuable as professionals.

This shift can bring up worries about job security as we question how relevant our skills will be in an AI-enhanced workplace. It can also affect how we feel about our work—success increasingly depends on how well we work with AI rather than just our individual abilities.

The key is to focus on what makes us uniquely human. By understanding what special value we bring and learning to partner effectively with AI, we can create new opportunities for innovation and professional growth. This isn't just about adapting to change—it's about finding new ways to make an impact.

Today's professional landscape looks very different from the past. Success comes from using AI to amplify our human capabilities, not replace them. When we combine human creativity, judgment, and adaptability with AI's power, we can tackle bigger challenges and achieve things that weren't possible before.

Think About This

  • How does working with AI affect how secure you feel in your job, and what can you do about any concerns?
  • What new professional goals become possible when you have AI as a partner?

 

Developing AI Self-Efficacy

How we see our future professional selves has a big impact on how well we actually use these tools. People who can picture themselves working successfully with AI are more likely to see it as an opportunity rather than a threat. This confidence in our ability to work effectively with AI—what we call AI self-efficacy—plays a crucial role in how well we adapt to these changes.

Building this confidence takes systematic effort. It means actively experimenting with AI tools, identifying ways AI can complement our skills, and gradually building our capabilities. By paying attention to how AI changes work patterns, we can make better decisions about which skills to develop while building the technical knowledge needed to use AI effectively.

The goal isn't just to become tech-savvy—it's about developing a professional identity that combines technological competence with human capabilities. This means staying in control of our professional development while actively shaping how AI fits into our work.

Think About This

  • What emotions come up for you when you think about working with AI?
  • What specific steps could you take to feel more comfortable and capable with AI tools?
  • How do you picture your future professional self in a workplace where AI is part of the team?

 

Conclusion: Embracing the Human-AI Future

“The urgent task before us is ensuring these transformations enhance rather than diminish human potential, creating workplaces where technology serves to elevate human capability rather than replace it. The decisions we make now, in these early days of AI integration, will shape not just the future of work, but the future of human agency in an AI-augmented world.“ Ethan Mollick

Bringing AI into the workplace gives us a chance to reimagine how we work together, innovate, and create value. When we combine human creativity and judgment with AI's capabilities, we can achieve things that neither could do alone.

But making this transition work takes careful thought and planning. As AI reshapes roles and workflows, we need to tackle challenges like adapting our skills, making ethical decisions, and keeping appropriate human oversight. Success comes from approaching these challenges with a growth mindset—embracing AI's opportunities while protecting the human elements that drive innovation and give our work meaning.

By building trust, staying adaptable, and sharing responsibility, we can create workplaces where AI truly partners with us, amplifying our strengths and opening up new possibilities. While this journey requires intention and collaboration, the reward—a more empowered and innovative workforce—makes it worth the effort.

Take Action Today

  1. Start Today: Begin experimenting with AI tools in your current role and explore relevant use cases
  2. Develop Your Framework: Create clear guidelines for AI usage within your team
  3. Invest in Learning: Commit to developing both domain expertise and AI literacy
  4. Lead the Change: Take an active role in shaping AI integration
  5. Build for the Future: Work with leadership to develop career pathways

 

Sources

entry.title: Your Path to SAP Integration Suite Certification: Courses, Exam Prep, and Expert Tips
entry.content:
Certifications are a great way to upskill and validate your expertise. If you are a developer and work within the integration space, the key certification to consider is the SAP Certified Associate - Integration Developer. This guide will tell you more about this exam and what to do to prepare.  

 

About the Exam 

Information about the exams, including the number of questions, cut scores and sample questions are published on learning.sap.com/certifications. The recommended certification for developers working in the integration space is the SAP Certified Associate - Integration Developer.  

The certification website gives you a breakdown of topic areas covered in the exam and their weight, so you know what to focus on. Check the exam page for the most up to date information. 

The certifications are valid for 12 months. You can extend the certification by 12 more months by completing an assessment. You will receive a heads up from SAP to let you know when it’s time to renew your certification. 

 

How to Schedule the Exam 

You can schedule your SAP Integration Suite certification exam in two ways: 

If You Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

With an SAP Learning Hub subscription, you receive four exam attempts per year as part of your plan. You can use these attempts to take up to four different certification exams or to retake an exam if needed. 

To schedule your exam: 

  1. Log in to learning.sap.com. 
  2. Find the certification exam you want to take. 
  3. Click Schedule. This will take you to the Questionmark certification portal, where you can view and manage your exam dates. 
  4. On the Questionmark certification portal, click Exam dashboard from the top-level navigation. Then click Schedule an exam.  
  5. Find the certification exam you want to take from the list (there are a lot, and it’s easier to do a control / command + F). Click the calendar icon to select the desired date and time. Then click Save Appointment. 
  6. To view your scheduled exams, click Exam Appointments from the top-level navigation.  

If You Don’t Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription, you can purchase a set of certification attempts.  

You have three purchase options: 

  • One certification attempt, available to purchase here. 
  • Two certification attempts, including 10 hours of practice system access with it, available to purchase here. 
  • A bundle of six attempts (usable for different certifications or exam retakes if needed), available to purchase here. 

Once purchased, follow the scheduling instructions on the website to book your exam. 

 

How to Stay Certified 

The SAP Integration Suite certification is valid for one year. To maintain your certification, you’ll need to renew it annually. 

  • If you have a Learning Hub subscription: You can renew your certification by completing a short assessment covering the latest SAP Integration Suite innovations. 
  • If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription: You must purchase a certification attempt and retake the full exam. You can do so here. 

For more details, visit the SAP certification renewal guide. 

 

What to Study for the SAP Integration Suite Certification: Learning Paths  

The best way to prepare for the exam is to take learning paths. Completing the learning paths along with their quizzes and hands on exercises will provide the knowledge and practice needed to pass the certification. Below are the recommended learning paths to pass the SAP Integration Suite certification exam: 

  • Managing Clean Core for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 
  • Developing with SAP Integration Suite learning path  

The learning paths take about 12 hours to complete, and they cover all relevant topics you need to pass the exam.  

 

Additional Resources to Support Your Learning 

While the learning paths cover all the important topics required to pass your certification exam, it’s always helpful to reinforce your learning with additional resources and hands-on practice. Here are some valuable materials to deepen your understanding of SAP Integration Suite and boost your confidence before the exam. 

Live Sessions – SAP Integration Suite 

Join expert-led live sessions on SAP Integration Suite to get hands-on guidance, ask questions, and stay updated on the latest developments. See the upcoming live sessions here. You need the Learning Hub subscription to access these sessions. 

SAP CodeJams 

Participate in free CodeJam sessions, where you can practice SAP Integration Suite in a collaborative, hands-on environment. These sessions are ideal for gaining real-world experience and troubleshooting with experts. More info here. 

 

SAP Integration Suite Missions & Tutorials 

Explore interactive missions and tutorials on the SAP Developers site. These step-by-step guides will help you apply what you’ve learned and build confidence before taking the certification exam. 

 

Your SAP Integration Suite Exam Prep Checklist 

Follow these steps to maximize your chances of passing the exam on your first attempt. 

  1. Understand the exam structure.  
    • Review the certification webpage to learn about the exam topics and their weight, so you know where to focus your studies. 
    • Familiarize yourself with the exam format, including the number of questions, time limit, and passing score. 
  2. Follow the recommended learning journey 
    • Complete all the recommended learning paths outlined in this post. 
    • Make sure to complete all hands-on activities and quizzes to reinforce your understanding. 
    • Alternatively, attend a classroom training to prepare for the certification. There, you will get access to a real system to complete exercises on your own. See the training page for upcoming classroom sessions. 
  3. Get more hands-on experience 
    • Practice using SAP Integration Suite in different scenarios. For example, you can complete missions in the discovery center.   
    • Experiment with SAP Integration Suite features beyond the course exercises to deepen your understanding. 
  4. Create a study plan 
    • Set a realistic study schedule. Spread your learning over a few weeks rather than cramming at the last minute. 
  5. Take a Practice Test  
    • Use the sample questions provided by SAP to get a feel for the exam format and question style. 
  6. Rest well before the exam 
    • Avoid last-minute cramming. It’s better to be well-rested and focused. 
  7. Read Questions Carefully During the Exam 
    • Pay attention to keywords like "best," "most effective," or "first step." 
    • Eliminate obviously wrong answers before choosing the best one. 
    • If unsure, mark the question for review and return to it later. 

 

Share Your Certification Journey and Help Others Succeed 

Earning your SAP Integration Suite certification is a great achievement, and your experience can help others on the same path. Share your journey in the Integration Group by posting about your study strategies, favorite resources, and exam tips. Whether it’s a learning method that worked well for you or a resource you found particularly useful, your insights could make a big difference for those preparing for the exam.

entry.title: Hurdles / Pitfalls in End-User Training for Software Implementation Programs
entry.content:
We all know that when an organization decides to modernize its legacy systems for new technology, End-User Training becomes critical for the successful transformation. However, several pitfalls / hurdles can undermine the effectiveness of end-user training.

Based on my experience of leading End User Adoption activities for various system implementation programs (e.g., SAP, Oracle, Ariba, Microsoft Dynamics, custom applications and platforms, etc.), I will share the key hurdles to watch out for.

Training Content Not User-centric

We happen to draft end-user training content, which is too technical, too general, or unclear & irrelevant. For example, the end-user training decks just show a dump of some as-is workflow diagrams (prepared by technical team) along with random system screenshots. OR, the end-user training decks have many references to configurations, integrations, middleware, technical system rules, back-end automated activities, etc. Such training content clearly indicates that the content is not user-centric. Such training decks make the understanding of new system even harder for the end users.

To avoid this, we must do careful & quality Instructional Design to generate result-oriented learning experiences for corporate users, for example:

  • Drive the learning experiences by the objectives, drafted around the expected user behaviour linked to the execution of actual business operations in the new system.
  • Focus on real-world scenarios & actual business processes, plus do the required detailing of Concepts, New Terminology, & Processes.
  • Do easier scaffolding of information to draft simple, visual, and well-structured materials tailored to the audience.
  • Drive the content coverage and presentation from the perspective of how adults learn and how their learning psychology works.
  • Generate the focus and reinforcement areas for key learning takeaways throughout the learning experiences.
  • Achieve user-engagement through various creative ways, e.g., content gamification, hands-on experiences, real-life scenarios, etc. to keep the learners hooked to the content and push the required learning.

Remember that if the training sessions are boring or irrelevant, users may lose interest and not absorb the information.

Training Content Not Tailored to Roles

Learning Consultants tend to think of one-size-fits all training, and ignore different roles and skill levels while drafting the content. To avoid this, we can tailor training to account for different user groups or job roles and workflows across departments in the client’s organization. Generally, the end-user training for new system implementation needs to encompass various roles, such as Warehouse workers, Finance teams, Procurement staff, etc.

The role-based training will educate end-users only the features that they need, for example, Accounts Payable (AP) Staff will learn invoice processing, and Finance Managers will learn Reporting tools.

If the same generic training is given to all users, employees will struggle to find relevant information in the training content, and may skip the training sessions or get disengaged. This will eventually increase errors after go-live, causing operational delays.

Poor Planning and Timing for End-User Training

Generally, it is observed that end-user training sessions are scheduled too early, which makes the end-users forget what they have learned before go-live. For example, SAP training was delivered 3-4 months before go-live. By the time employees need to use the system, they forget most of what they learned.

On the other hand, if we conduct training sessions too late, it leads to the rushed sessions before go-live, or we squeeze the training into a short timeframe. This way, we do not allow enough time for hands-on practice and feedback.

Poor planning and timing will lead to the increased support tickets overwhelming the IT Support Team and Functional SAP Consultants. This eventually will drop the productivity as employees will struggle to complete tasks.

As a solution, we can conduct the training closer to go-live, offer refresher sessions post implementation, and provision just-in-time performance support knowledge base.

Inadequate Training Methods

While executing the end-user training sessions, we over-rely on passive training methods, for example, PowerPoint-driven instructor sessions / lectures, instead of interactive learning, or modern learning approaches like micro-learning, gamification, self-paced modules, on-demand videos, etc.

For ideal results, we can creatively use a blended approach, for example, self-paced modules, live demos, hands-on practice, self-help performance support material, etc.

Also, if we do not provision a sandbox environment / interactive hands-on practice for users to practice safely, this leads to poor user-adoption. If the training is more theoretical using PowerPoint presentations, rather than live demonstrations or sandbox environment, end-users will not get the confidence and will struggle with actual transactions during go-live.

Failure to Address Change Resistance

If the end-users do not feel positive about the change and carry the change-resistance in their minds, they will not get engaged in the training experiences, and will not take the training seriously. Hence, the organization should not assume that employees would embrace the new system (e.g., SAP) because the leadership mandated it.

If there is no clear communication on why the change is happening or how it will benefit the users, they will not make serious efforts to acquire the learning. We need to remember that negative attitude from users will make the adoption even harder.

As a solution, we need to implement a change management strategy, with leadership involvement and user champions to drive the adoption. The change management efforts should go hand-in-hand with the end-user adoption efforts.

No Change Champions or Super Users

Organizations treat training as a one-time event rather than ongoing process, and they undermine the need of Change Champions and Super Users to support and reinforce the ongoing learning.

As Learning Consultant, we need to guide the client on the role of Change Champions and Super Users, and how the client can identify & qualify them. For example, Change Champions and Super Users should have strong understanding the system and processes; they should be involved in various implementation activities (e.g., design sessions, system demos, testing, etc.) right from the beginning.

No Post-training Support

Generally, I have observed that once the training is done, users have no-easy way to get help when they face problems. Also, the IT/Helpdesk teams are not prepared to answer the questions.

The post-training support must be in place to ensure the long-term success. If we do not offer follow-up sessions, refresher sessions, on-demand learning materials & support; FAQs, Quick Reference Guides, and ongoing learning opportunities, it will weaken the transfer of learning to real-life. This will eventually overwhelm the Helpdesk with calls and tickets.

Here, as a solution, we can offer ongoing support, including quick reference guides, advanced performance support framework & knowledge base; on-demand assistance by Change Champions and Super Users, dedicated Support Members for tricky SAP modules (e.g., Finance transactions) for troubleshooting; dedicated Help Desk to assist with adoption issues, and so on.

Failure to Measure Training Effectiveness

For training effectiveness, we rely only on attendance and do not assess real knowledge retention and transfer. For example, we do not have a mechanism to measure actual user understanding; no follow-up surveys or feedback loops for improve training, no metrics to track the post-training performance to measure adoption, etc.

As a solution, we can evaluate effectiveness through assessments, surveys, usage metrics, etc.

Conclusion

It is essential to avid the above pitfalls / hurdles to generate the effective end-user training experiences.
